**Ciołek** ([Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") for "[bull calf](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle")") is a [Polish coat of arms](/wiki/Polish_heraldry "Polish heraldry"), one of the oldest in [medieval Poland](/wiki/Medieval_Poland "Medieval Poland").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It was used by many *[szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")* (noble) families under the late [Piast dynasty](/wiki/Piast_dynasty "Piast dynasty"), under the [Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Lithuanian_Commonwealth "Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth"), during the [Partitions of Poland](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), and in the 20th century. The variant names "Siolek" and "Cialek" arose from miscommunication among early-20th-century [Polish immigrants to the United States](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cio%C5%82ek_coat_of_arms&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

A history of this coat-of-arms is included in a *[Short History of Polish Arms](/w/index.php?title=Short_History_of_Polish_Arms&action=edit&redlink=1 "Short History of Polish Arms (page does not exist)")* written by Countess Ewa Theresa Korab-Karpinska in the late 1980s, which is currently lodged at the [College of Arms](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms") in London. As heraldic heiress and only daughter of Tadeusz Josef Żeleński (also spelled Zielinski/Zelinski), Ewa was one of a handful of women to write upon the subject of heraldry. The bull and crown were later exceptionally incorporated as a crest into an English [Grant of Arms](/wiki/Grant_of_arms "Grant of arms") by the English College of Arms.
